coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence emission from carbon quantum dot-sulfite coreactant system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yongqiang
Chen, Congqiang
Lin, Jianpeng
Zhou, Nana
Chi, Yuwu
Chen, Guonan
coredata.dc:description:
A new electrochemiluminescence (ECL) system consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and sulfite
SO
3
2 -
has been reported. In the ECL system,
SO
3
2 -
is a new ECL coreactant that can significantly enhance the cathodic ECL signal of CQDs. When cycling the potential between −1.300 and +1.400V,
SO
3
2 -
is firstly electrochemically oxidized to sulfur trioxide anion free radical (
SO
3
-
) in the anodic polarization process. Then the electrogenerated
SO
3
-
is involved in a three-step auto-catalytic reaction in the presence of dissolved oxygen, producing sulfate radical (
SO
4
-
). In the subsequent cathodic polarization process, CQDs are electrochemically reduced to negatively charged CQDs (C −). Finally, the electrogenerated C − species react with the
SO
4
-
to form excited-state CQDs (C∗), emitting light (575nm) when they come back to their ground states.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL response of the CQD-SO3 2− system
ECL mechanism of the CQD-SO3 2− system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the system used for electrochemical and ECL measurement.

(a) HRTEM images of the obtained CQDs; (b) FTIR spectra of the obtained CQDs; (c) UV absorption and FL spectra (recorded for progressively longer excitation wavelengths in 20nm increments) of the obtained CQD solution; (d) optical photograph under excitation of 365nm of obtained CQD solution.

Cyclic voltammogram (A) and the corresponding ECL potential curves (B) of PBS blank (a), 0.200mgmL−1 CQD (b), 1.00×10−3 molL−1 Na2SO3 (c), and 0.200mgmL−1 CQD+1.00×10−3 molL−1 Na2SO3 (d) in 0.100molL−1 air-saturated PBS (scan rate: 500mVs−1). Inset: amplified ECL responses of PBS blank, 0.200mgmL−1 CQD and 1.00×10−3 molL−1 Na2SO3, respectively.

(A) ECL response of different concentration CQD in the presence of 1.00×10−3 molL−1 Na2SO3; (B) ECL response of different concentration Na2SO3 in the presence of 0.400mgmL−1 CQD in 0.100molL−1 air-saturated PBS (scan rate: 500mVs−1; potential window: −1.300V∼+1.400V).

ECL spectrum for the (a) CQD-Na2SO3 (0.400mgmL−1 CQDs; 1.00molL−1 Na2SO3; PBS), (b) CQD-K2S2O8 (0.400mgmL−1 CQDs; 0.100molL−1 K2S2O8; 1.00molL−1 KNO3; PBS) coreactant ECL systems. 1Hz potential steps between +1.800 and −1.500V were applied for the CQD-
S
2
O
8
2 -
system, 1Hz potential steps between +1.400 and −1.300V were applied for the CQD-SO3 2− system.

(A) Effect of upper limit of potential window on the ECL intensity of 0.400mgmL−1 CQD and 1.00×10−3 molL−1 Na2SO3 in air-saturated PBS (scan rate: 500mVs−1; lower limit of potential window: −1.300V). (B) A typical ECL transient response (lower curve) of CQD by stepping potential (upper curve) between +1.800V and a given negative potential. (C) Effect of negative potential on the ECL transients of CQD in pH 7.0 PBS.
Schematic diagram for the ECL reaction mechanism of CQD-SO3 2− system.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence emission from carbon quantum dot-sulfite coreactant system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fujian 350108, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A new electrochemiluminescence (ECL) system consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and sulfite
SO
3
2 -
has been reported. In the ECL system,
SO
3
2 -
is a new ECL coreactant that can significantly enhance the cathodic ECL signal of CQDs. When cycling the potential between −1.300 and +1.400V,
SO
3
2 -
is firstly electrochemically oxidized to sulfur trioxide anion free radical (
SO
3
-
) in the anodic polarization process. Then the electrogenerated
SO
3
-
is involved in a three-step auto-catalytic reaction in the presence of dissolved oxygen, producing sulfate radical (
SO
4
-
). In the subsequent cathodic polarization process, CQDs are electrochemically reduced to negatively charged CQDs (C −). Finally, the electrogenerated C − species react with the
SO
4
-
to form excited-state CQDs (C∗), emitting light (575nm) when they come back to their ground states.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanocrystals with diameters less than 10nm [1], which have recently attracted more and more attention due to their fascinating optical properties, such as photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, chemiluminescence and especially the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [1–4]. ECL is a form of chemiluminescence in which the light emitting chemiluminescent reaction is preceded by an electrochemical reaction [5]. ECL shows many advantages, including high versatility, simplified optical setup, good temporal and spatial control and very low background signal [6,7], and has attracted increasing attention in chemical detection, biosensing, imaging and electron-transfer (ET) probing [5–9]. Traditionally, ECL luminophores are metal–organic complexes and organics, typically,
Ru ( bpy
)
3
2 +
and its derivatives, and luminol [5]. These luminophores exhibit excellent ECL activities. However, difficult labeling has seriously limited their applications. Recently, a new group of ECL luminophores, quantum dots (QDs), have attracted more and more attention [9], since they not only exhibit excellent ECL activities but also have the merit of easy labeling, and thus have promising applications in bio-sensing and cell-imaging. Since the ECL behavior of Si nanoparticles was first reported in 2002 [10], many studies have been focused on the mechanisms and applications of the ECL of QDs [10–15]. However, semiconductor-based QDs, especially, the most studied Cd-containing QDs [11–15], have raised concerns over their stability, biocompatibility and environmental toxicity [16]. CQDs, a kind of emerging ECL luminophores, have several obvious advantages over the previously reported QDs, such as excellent chemical inertness, easy labeling and low toxicity. Furthermore, CQDs can be easily obtained due to the fact that many facile methods have been developed to prepare CQDs [1–4,17–23]. In brief, CQDs would be very promising ECL alternatives to the semiconductor-based QDs and the conventional molecular ECL reagents such as
Ru ( bpy
)
3
2 +
. However, up to date, little attention has been paid to the ECL study of CQDs [4,17,18], especially, few coreactants (only
S
2
O
8
2 -
in fact) have been found to produce ECL signals with CQDs. Apparently, this has seriously limited further applications of CQDs in ECL. Therefore, discovering and studying new coreactants for CQDs’ ECL should be very important in both revealing CQD-based ECL mechanisms, and enlarging the applications of CQDs’ ECL in the future.

Herein, it was found that sulfite (
SO
3
2 -
) could serve as a new coreactant of CQDs in ECL reactions and greatly enhance the weak cathodic ECL emission of CQDs. It was revealed that the ECL response of the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system was highly dependent on the oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
, the reduction of CQDs and the presence of dissolved oxygen. On this basis, a detailed ECL reaction mechanism of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system has been proposed.

Wood based activated carbon powder (200mesh) and Na2SO3 (⩾99.9%) were purchased from Fuchen chemical reagent company (Tianjin, China). 1 and 30kDa molecular weight cut off (MWCO) membranes (Amicon Ultra-4, Millipore) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company. Other chemicals were analytically pure and used as received. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were prepared by neutralizing 0.100molL−1 phosphoric acid solution with a concentrated sodium hydroxide solution (5.00molL−1) to pH 7.0.

CQDs were extracted from wood based activated carbon (AC) powder by a previously reported method [17]. Briefly, 1g of AC powder was refluxed with 100mL of 4.00molL−1 HNO3 for 24h, and then the resulting suspension was dried under vacuum to remove HNO3. The obtained black solid was dispersed into 50mL doubly distilled water and centrifuged (2770g) for 30min to remove the non-fluorenscent deposit. Finally, the CQD supernatant was ultra-filtered in turn by 1 and 30kDa MWCO membranes and the components with particle weight between 1 and 30kDa were collected and used for further study.

High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were recorded on an electronic microscope (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV). The infrared (IR) spectrum of dried CQDs was obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). ECL and electrochemical measurements were carried out on an ECL detection system (MPI-E, Remex Electroic Instrument Ltd. Co., Xi’an, China) equipped with a home-made ECL cell. As shown in Fig. 1 , the ECL cell consisted of a glass cup, a silicon rubber plug with a gas inlet and a gas outlet, and a three-electrode system, i.e., a Pt wire (with area of 0.31cm2) working electrode (WE), a Pt wire counter electrode (CE) and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE). The half part of the cup bottom below the counter electrode was covered with a light barrier to remove possible interfering light emitting from the counter electrode during the ECL measurements. Before measurements, the platinum wire working electrode and counter electrode were cleaned by ultrasonication. ECL spectrum measurements were carried on a Fluorescence Steady State and TCSPC Fluorescence Lifetime Spectro-Fluorimeter (FLS920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) combined with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C, CH Instruments, USA).

CQDs were extracted from wood-based activated carbon according to a method described elsewhere [17]. As shown in Fig. 1, the as-prepared CQDs (1–30kDa) are 3–5nm sp2 carbon crystals with a (100) lattice parameter of 0.212nm (Fig. 2 a). The CQDs exhibit very good solubility in water due to the abundant oxygen-containing groups (–OH group at 3410cm−1, CO group at 1700cm−1, –COO– group at 1610 and 1380cm−1) at their surfaces (Fig. 2b). The CQD solution shows a broad absorption band below 550nm with a peak centered at 275nm (Fig. 2c). The CQD solution has excitation-dependent emission spectra (Fig. 2c) and emits yellow fluorescence under the excitation of 365nm UV light (Fig. 2d). The excitation-dependent emission may be associated with the C–OH, the aromatic CC and the CO group functional groups in the GQDs [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL response of the CQD-SO3 2− system
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 3 , when the potential is scanned between +1.400 and −1.300V in PBS (pH 7.0), the CQD solution shows no obvious electrochemical response, and produces only a very weak cathodic ECL signal.
SO
3
2 -
shows an obvious anodic peak around +0.7V, but produces no any observable ECL signal. The mixed solution of CQDs and
SO
3
2 -
gives no new electrochemical signal except for the oxidation peak of
SO
3
2 -
, but exhibits strong cathodic ECL light emission in the range from −0.700 to −1.300V. The ECL response is affected by the concentrations of both CQDs and
SO
3
2 -
(see Fig. 4 ). The ECL intensity is increased when the concentration of the CQDs is increased from 0 to 0.600mgmL−1 (Fig. 4A). Especially, in the range from 0 to 0.400mgmL−1, the ECL intensity is linearly proportional to the concentration of CQDs. The ECL intensity is also enhanced as the concentration of
SO
3
2 -
is increased (Fig. 4B). There is a good linear relationship between the ECL intensity and the concentration of
SO
3
2 -
in the range of 1.00×10−5 to 1.00×10−3 molL−1. These results indicate that CQDs and SO3 2− can form an excellent coreactant ECL system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL mechanism of the CQD-SO3 2− system
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL spectra of CQD-SO3 2− system was measured and compared with that of CQD-S2O8 2− system (see Fig. 5 ), in order to identify the luminophore and further reveal the ECL mechanism. During measuring the ECL spectra, 1Hz potential steps between +1.800 and −1.500V were applied to stable ECL pulses. Meanwhile, the ECL emission spectra were recorded by the fluorescence spectrophotometer (scanned from 400 to 800nm with a scan rate of 2.5nm/s and a step of 0.5nm), from which the ECL maximum emission peaks can be obtained (Fig. 5). It should be noted here, the pulse potentials rather than the constant cathodic potentials should be applied to establish stable diffusion layers at the electrode surfaces and thus produce stable ECL emission, which is important for obtaining valid ECL spectra recorded by a relatively slow wavelength scan. The ECL spectrum of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system (Fig. 5a) is almost the same as that of CQD-
S
2
O
8
2 -
system (Fig. 5b), except for the difference in ECL intensity. The ECL spectra of the two systems have the same ECL maximum emission wavelength (575nm), suggesting that they share the same luminophore. It has been well studied that the CQD serves as the luminophores while
S
2
O
8
2 -
acts as the coreactant in the CQD-
S
2
O
8
2 -
system [4,17,18]. Apparently, in the present CQD-
SO
3
2 -
ECL system, the CQD and
SO
3
2 -
should act as the luminophore and the coreactant, respectively.

The applied potential was found very important for the ECL response of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system. Although the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system produces a cathodic ECL signal only in the range from −0.700 to −1.300V, an anodic polarization is essential for the ECL. For example, when the potential is cathodically polarized from 0 to –1.300V, the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system only produces a very weak cathodic ECL signal (curve (a) in Fig. S1), which is similar with the CQD system in the absence of the coreactant,
SO
3
2 -
, (see curve (b) in Fig. S1). In contrast, when the potential is first anodically polarized (from 0 to +1.400V) and then cathodically polarized (from +1.400 to –1.300V), a strong cathodic ECL signal can be observed at potential <−0.7V (curve (c) in Fig. S1). These results indicate that a pre-oxidation process is essential for the ECL of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system. In the potential range of −1.300 and +1.400V, CQDs show nearly no observable redox peaks, whereas
SO
3
2 -
has an irreversible oxidation peak around +0.7V (Fig. 3A). Therefore, the oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
should be necessary in the ECL reaction of the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system. Apparently, the upper limit of potential window will affect the oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
, and then affected the cathodic ECL intensity. As shown in Fig. 6 A, when the upper limit of potential window is gradually increased from +0.600 to +1.400V (while keeping the lower limit of potential window at −1.300V), the cathodic ECL signal is increased gradually to the maximum at +1.400V. However, further increase in the upper limit of potential window leads to a gradual decrease in ECL intensity. The increase in ECL in the range of +0.600 to +1.400V is consistent with the increase in the electrooxidation current of
SO
3
2 -
shown in Fig. 3A, once again showing that the ECL of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system is associated with the electrochemical oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
. The decrease in ECL at the potential higher than +1.400V might be explained by that large amount of oxygen bubbles are produced from the electrolysis of H2O under the high potential condition, which greatly promotes the diffusion of the electro-oxidation products or intermediates of
SO
3
2 -
, and thus decreases their concentration around the electrode surface, then finally decreases the ECL signal. In order to find out the oxidation products or the intermediates of
SO
3
2 -
involved in the ECL reactions of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system,
SO
3
2 -
was pre-oxidized at +1.400V using a constant potential mode, after which ECL activity of the oxidation products was investigated in the presence of CQDs by cycling the potential between 0 and −1.300V. Experimental results indicate that the final oxidation products of
SO
3
2 -
can’t enhance the cathodic ECL response of CQDs. This suggests some unstable intermediates (most likely free radicals) electrogenerated from
SO
3
2 -
are involved in the ECL reactions. It has been previously reported that the oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
can produce sulfur trioxide anion free radicals (
SO
3
-
) (reaction 1) and then further yield a peroxyl free radical (O3SOO −) in the presence of molecular oxygen (O2) (reaction 2), after which two possible propagation steps proceed auto-catalytically (reactions 3, 4), producing abundant sulfate radicals (
SO
4
-
) [25]. The
SO
4
-
has been demonstrated to be an active free radical that can react with negatively charged CQDs (C −) to produce strong light emission [4,17,18]. Apparently, O2 should be an important factor in the formation of
SO
4
-
. Accordingly, the effect of dissolved oxygen (DO) on the ECL response of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system was investigated. In the experiment of investigating effect of DO on the ECL response of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system, the potential was scanned between −1.300 and +1.000V in order to avoid the electro-generation of O2 at high potential. The CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system only produces a very weak ECL signal in the absence of DO whereas gives rise to strong ECL emission by bubbling oxygen into the solution (Fig. S2). These results prove that DO is essential for the ECL reaction of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system, and suggest that
SO
4
-
should be the oxidation product of
SO
3
2 -
involved in the ECL reaction.
(1)
SO
3
2 -
-
e
-
→
SO
3
-
(2)
SO
3
-
+
O
2
→
O
3
SOO
-
(3)
O
3
SOO
-
+
SO
3
2 -
→
SO
4
-
+
SO
4
2 -
(4)
SO
4
-
+
SO
3
2 -
→
SO
4
2 -
+
SO
3
-

In addition to the above mentioned effect of the higher limit of potential window, the effect of the lower limit of potential window on the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
ECL can be known from Fig. 3B. The ECL of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
begins to be observed when lower limit of potential window is more negative than −0.600V, increases significantly between −0.800 and −1.200V, and tends to reach a maximum value at −1.300V (see Fig. 3B). The potential-dependence of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
ECL is very similar with that of CQDs ECL in the absence of
SO
3
2 -
(curves (b) and (d) in Fig. 3B), except that the ECL intensity of the former is much larger than the latter. These results imply that the low potential limit affects the ECL response of the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system by affecting the reduction efficiency of CQDs. To further verify this viewpoint, the reduction of CQDs at different negative potentials in the absence of
SO
3
2 -
was monitored by measuring ECL transients of CQDs (see Fig. 6B and C). In experiment, we applied 1Hz potential steps between +1.8V, where positively charged CQDs (C +) may be produced, and a given negative potential, where C − may be produced (see Fig. 6B), and then we measured the ECL signal resulting from electron transfer reaction between the C − and the C + [17]. Obviously, in this ECL system, ECL intensity should be proportional to the reduction efficiency of CQD at various negative potentials since the amounts of C + produced at the initial potential step (+1.800V for 1s) are the same. The experimental results indicate that CQDs begin to be reduced around −0.600V, and are reduced most efficiently when the potential is lower than −1.300V (see Fig. 6C). The fact that the potential-dependence of CQDs’ reduction is consistent with the potential-dependence of ECL intensity indicates that reduction of CQDs is necessary for the ECL emission of CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system.

Based on the discussed results mentioned above, a possible ECL mechanism has been proposed for the CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system. As shown in Fig. 7 , in the anodic polarization process,
SO
3
2 -
is oxidized to SO3
− at the working electrode. Subsequently, a three-step auto-catalytic reaction is initiated by the electrogenerated
SO
3
-
: (1) The dissolved oxygen (O2) in the solution reacts with
SO
3
-
to produce O3SOO −. (2) The produced O3SOO − reacts with
SO
3
2 -
, generating
SO
4
2 -
and
SO
4
-
. (3) The generated SO4
− reacts with
SO
3
2 -
to yield
SO
4
2 -
and SO3
−. In the following cathodic polarization process, the CQD (C) is reduced to a negatively charged particle (C −). Finally, the electron transfer between
SO
4
-
generated in the autocatalytic reaction and the electro-generated C − produces the excited-state CQD (C∗) [4], and gives rise to the strong ECL emission when C∗ goes back to its ground state (C). It should be pointed out that the
SO
4
-
could be produced all the time via the three-step auto-catalytic reaction. Therefore, although the lifetime of
SO
4
-
was very short,
SO
4
-
could be still present in the cathodic polarization process and reacted with the C − species to produce luminescence emission.

A new and excellent CQD-
SO
3
2 -
coreactant ECL system that can produce strong cathodic ECL signal has been found. The electrochemical oxidation of
SO
3
2 -
, the presence of DO and the reduction of CQDs were found to be three key factors for the ECL reaction of this coreactant ECL system. ECL mechanism in the system CQD-
SO
3
2 -
system has been proposed. This coreactant ECL system is another CQD-based ECL system since the first CQD ECL was reported in 2009, which would be helpful for finding other new and excellent ECL systems, understanding reaction mechanisms of CQD-based ECL, and extending their applications in chemical sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21075018), National Basic Research Program of China (Grant 2010CB732400), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Chinese University (NCET-10-0019) and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2012.12.086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

Structure and bonding: semiconductor and nanoparticles

Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence of CdTe nanoparticles
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence from a Cdse nanocrystal film and its sensing application in aqueous solution
Electrochemiluminescence sensors for scavengers of hydroxyl radical based on its annihilation in CdSe quantum dots film/peroxide system

Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon

Wet chemistry route to hydrophobic blue fluorescent nanodiamond
Fingerprinting photoluminescence of functional groups in graphene oxide
Sulfate anion free radical formation by the peroxidation of (Bi)sulfite and its reaction with hydroxyl radical scavengers
